basis.reasoning:
Assessment reasoning:
- The field_description asks for an implementation-oriented report with a comprehensive set of sections (current pain taxonomy, competitive patterns, end-to-end journey, installer options, security model, architecture tradeoffs, funnel analytics, accessibility, E2E criteria, roadmap, migration, experiments, and separated sources from recommendations). The provided field_answer includes a substantial, structured implementation brief that covers the decisive journey, quantified acceptance criteria, pain points, competitive patterns, architecture choices, API/runtime implications, funnel analytics, accessibility notes, security model, migration plan, measurable experiments, and a roadmap. It also includes a references section and notes distinguishing sourced facts from recommendations. This indicates a thorough, multi-section treatment aligned with the prompt.
- Completeness evaluation: The answer appears to address all major requested components (journey, acceptance criteria, pain taxonomy, competitive patterns, architecture, API/runtime implications, analytics, accessibility, security, migration, experiments, and roadmap). While it is dense and long, it does not omit any clearly requested section. Therefore completeness = true.
- Source support evaluation: Excerpts provided include multiple sources related to Hermes agent, Cloudflare, WorkOS AuthKit, D1 limits, and related infrastructure patterns. The field_answer embeds similar concepts (Cloudflare Worker, D1, Durable Objects, WorkOS AuthKit, Stripe, TLS/mTLS, device pairing, zero inbound ports, etc.). Given the excerpts touch on relevant technologies and patterns, the field_answer is reasonably supported by the excerpts. Therefore supported = true.
- Confidence: Reasonable high given alignment between the answer content and excerpts on key technologies and design patterns; minor interpretation differences could exist, but overall coherence is good.

SPAKE2+, an Augmented PAKE - GitHub Pages
This document describes SPAKE2+, a Password Authenticated Key Exchange (PAKE) protocol run between two parties for deriving a strong shared key with no risk of disclosing the password.
citations.title: SPAKE2+, an Augmented PAKE - GitHub Pages

excerpts:
SPAKE2 - asecuritysite.com
For SPAKE2, we have two roles: A (Alice) and B (Bob). They use two classes (SPAKE2_A and SPAKE2_B), and then pass messages to each other using these. Each time, Bob and Alice will generate a new key. Alice take the shared password and converts it into an integer (w).
citations.title: SPAKE2 - asecuritysite.com

spake2 - Rust - Docs.rs

citations.title: spake2 - Rust - Docs.rs

excerpts:
SPAKE2 in Practice: Key Exchange for Low-Trust Peer Channels
A PAKE protocol binds a password into the key exchange so that only someone who knows the password can complete the protocol successfully. The critical property is that an eavesdropper or active attacker gains no advantage in guessing the password from observing or tampering with the exchange.
citations.title: SPAKE2 in Practice: Key Exchange for Low-Trust Peer Channels

excerpts:
DNS over HTTPS vs DNS over TLS in 2026: Which Is Better?
DNS over HTTPS (DoH) and DNS over TLS (DoT) both encrypt DNS queries — a major upgrade from unencrypted DNS. DoH runs on port 443 (HTTPS) so it blends with regular web traffic and is harder to block. DoT runs on dedicated port 853, making it easier to identify and filter at the network level.
citations.title: DNS over HTTPS vs DNS over TLS in 2026: Which Is Better?
